Hippocrates was one of the first to document and advocate for things like aromatherapy, appropriate use of medication and surgical procedures, and various forms of movement to help the body heal naturally. Physical therapist as Movement Specialists The current APTA Vision Statement is: "Transforming society by optimizing movement to improve the human experience." While movement is only one piece of the puzzle, it is a big piece. Most of what you will do as a PT will be centered around helping people move more and body parts to move better. We do this through therapeutic exercises and manual techniques that we will cover in this class. The purpose of this module and course is to help you learn the foundational principles for how and why various exercises/treatments work. This course will not teach you all there is to know about exercises, nor will it show you every exercise/treatment out there. However, empowered by foundational knowledge, you will be able to begin problem solving and creating your own treatment plans for your patents. The following pages will help you understand how to apply these prinicples and how far we have come in our understanding since the time of Hippocrates. What is the value of prescribing therapeutic interventions? "The ultimate goal of a therapeutic exercise program is the achievement of an optimal level of symptom-free movement during basic to complex physical activities." (Colby & Kisner, page 1, ed 7) Patients and clients come into see a Physical Therapist (PT) because there is some part of their life that they are not able to fully participate in, but these activities are important to them. There is usually a physical impairment associated with these movement system disorders that is caused by injury, disease, or some health-related condition. Others seek out the advice of a PT because they would like to improve their overall fitness or quality of life while others seek advice for health promotion and wellness in order to reduce the risk of injury or disease. The PT is ideal for helping each of these groups of people because the PT is a movement specialist. PTs tailor therapeutic intervention programs to help the patient achieve this optimal level of symptom-free movement. These are best done by crafting a personalized treatment plan based on what the patient needs to improve their quality of life.     Using clinical reasoning to tailor therapeutic intervention programs A little more than 1/3 of the time in PT school is devoted to foundational science, such as Anatomy, PT Exam Skills, EBP, Neuroscience, Integumentary, etc. As a clinician, you will use these foundation courses to know the different forms of exercise, how different exercises affect the different tissues of the body, how to appropriately load tissues for a desired goal, apply and integrate

It was a turning point in our profession to be labeled the movement specialist. This is why our goal in working with patients is to help them get back to the movements they need to be able to participate in the activities that are most important to them. The APTA's Vision Statement for our Profession is, "Transforming society by optimizing movement to improve the human experience," which reflects our specialty across all practices of Physical Therapy.   The human movement system is a balance of each of the systems of the body seen to the left. These body systems interact to control each of the elements of physical function that were on the prior page, such as balance, muscle performance, etc. Additionally, the body systems react, adapt, and develop in response to forces and physical stresses placed on the tissues that make up component parts of movement.   We will learn how to appropriately stress the body systems to improve physical function. Some stresses are good or normal. For example, gravity helps the body maintain functional levels of strength while gentle movements to a joint help it maintain mobility after a surgery. Using this idea of appropriate stress for the tissues in context of therapeutic exercise interventions allows us to have basis for selecting certain exercises at certain loads. We decide how much force and load to place on the human movement system, taking into account specific tissues or individual structures in a controlled environment. From there, we can progress or regress the exercises in a safe manner to improve movement, getting the patient better, faster.

Excessive load or forces can cause acute injuries, like sprains, strains, or fractures. Excessive loads can also cause chronic conditions, like stress reactions in bones or tendinopathies.   Insufficient loads can lead to degeneration, degradation, or deformity. An extreme example of this is astronauts in space. The lack of gravity and the long time that astronauts spend on the Space Station leads to significant muscle atrophy and loss of bone mineral density. NASA has had to come up with creative ways to help astronauts impose these stresses on their bodies in order to minimize damage.           Now that we have talked about being Movement Specialists addressing Movement System Impairments, let's revisit the ICF Model. You may also see the terms primary impairment, secondary impairment, and composite impairment used. These are helpful terms to consider, especially with long-term therapeutic intervention planning.   Primary, or direct, impairments arise directly from a health condition or injury. Your textbook gives an example of a patient referred to PT with a medical diagnosis of rotator cuff tendinopathy. The patient might exhibit primary impairments of body function, such as pain, limited ROM, weakness of specific shoulder girdle muscles or glenohumeral muscles, etc. Secondary, or indirect, impairments occur as a sequela to the primary impairments or occur in other body systems. The patient may present with postural impairments or faulty movement patterns of the head, upper extremity, or trunk that are caused by the pain and limited range of motion, not directly due to the tendon pathology. Composite impairments are the result of multiple underlying causes (a combination of Primary and Secondary impairments). A patient may tear a ligament in their ankle and be immobilized for several weeks. They may demonstrate impaired balance due to chronic ligamentous laxity (body structure) and impaired ankle proprioception and weakness (body function) due to immobilization/disuse. Why does this matter with my treatment planning? Often times, treating the primary impairment can help the patient feel better in the moment. Only focusing on primary impairments does not focus on the root cause of the symptoms. The patient may plateau in their progress early on, and it can be frustrating for both the patient and the therapist!   Addressing secondary impairments early and concurrently helps address "the cause of the cause of impairments." By treating the underlying cause of the patient's main complaint, you are going to get them better faster, and you have a better chance of keeping the impairment from returning.   A good example is a patient that has low back pain secondary to hypermobility at L4-5 and a medical diagnosis of stenosis at that level. When watching the patient walk, you notice that they stand at their full height, but their pelvis remains in an anterior pelvic tilt. Additionally, the patient's hip does not extend well when they are walking. You do your evaluation, and you confirm hypermobility at L4-5. When assessing the hip, you notice decreased joint mobility in the hip, particularly with anterior glide (goes with hip extension, but you will learn this in Movement Science 1). If you try to stabilize L4-5 with all the right BP Cuff exercises, progression into weight-bearing and standing, address transitional movements, etc., you can get the patient's pain down. However, you are going to be more effective if you address the hip hypermobility at the same time you address the lumbar hypomobility. Without also treating the hip, the patient's symptoms will likely return. To improve efficiency, you can progress the treatment to address both of these regions with the patient standing and walking. This will improve the patient and help them return to their prior level of function by doing an exercise that is functional.

A composite impairment happens when both primary and secondary impairments are the source of the composite impairments. The example from the textbook is the composite impairment of loss of balance. The case involves a patient who severely sprained the ankle in a traumatic injury, resulting in a torn anterior talofibular ligament. and subsequent follow-up with a walking boot. The primary impairments could be loss of proprioception, muscle weakness, and loss of ankle dorsiflexion from being in the boot. The secondary impairments would be increased lateral joint laxity, pain, and edema from the tear.     The Principles Behind Comprehensive Patient Management To better understand the Patient Management Model, the principles that make up the model need to be reviewed because they are embedded in each phase of the patient management model. Be sure to review pages 12-14 (7th ed.) for further details and examples as needed.   Borrowed from: https://www.indeed.com/career-advice/career-development/critical-thinking-examples Critical Thinking Clinical decision-making is the art or critical thinking but in the clinical setting, typically with a patient focus. The infographic to the left puts critical thinking into steps or skills that help overcome challenges. These steps may be views as: 1) Observation, 2) Analysis, 3) Inference, 4) communication, and 5) problem-solving.   Using a real-life example, we can work through these. Example: you walk into a room and the lights are off. 1) what do you observe? - you notice the lights are of, you need light so this is a problem, the solution is to get light. 2) What data do you need to overcome the problem? - you could see if the light switch works, you could see if there is a bulb in the light fixture 3) What can you infer from the data gathered? If the light switch does not work, then you may draw the conclusion that there is an issue with the switch or wiring. If there is no light bulb, you know from personal experience that a bulb is needed to produce light 4) Who do I need to communicate about the issue to overcome the problem? you could reach out to your landlord, facility services, or the hardware store to determine in parts are available. 5) How do I overcome this problem? So far you have identified the issue, you have found the source of the problem, you have thought of a potential solution, you have communicated the problem, now you work to put it all together and to test out the problem through troubleshooting.     This is a bit of a basic example, but hopefully, this structured process can help you when you are stuck. If you apply this step-by- step thinking to patient care and competency exams, then you can process through any situation.

In order to do this, therapists need to be able to merge clarification and understanding with critical and creative thinking.   An example that relates to this class is the misconception that PTs just need to follow a "recipe" of exercises. An example of this would be the common prescription of Bridges and Clams for any and all hip or knee issues. Using the process above, you can think about why a PT may have selected these exercises. Was it to target certain tissues? Was it to get the gluteal muscles to work without the back muscles doing all of the work? It is these clinical decisions that make our exercise prescriptions skilled PT services.   You may hear a PT talk about CPRs or CPGs. These are Clinical Prediction Rules or Guidelines. They are tools that we can use for specific issues like low back pain. The tool is a summation of evidence and gives the reader an idea of what treatment plans may be effective or less likely to succeed.       Optional Activity: The following is a sorting activity of examples of circumstances where it is appropriate for PTs to communicate and coordinate care of the patient with another provider.        Coordination, Communication, and Documentation Coordination As PTs, we are responsible for coordinating the care for patients who seek PT services. We decide if the patient would be best seen by a PT or PTA, whether a patient is appropriate for PT services, and when the patient is ready for discharge. In different settings, we work with a team to provide input on appropriate discharge locations, adaptive and assistive devices/technology needs, and even recommending other services or tests that the patient may need. We often will work on scheduling patients and managing the day-to-day flow of our schedules. Communication We'll review this more towards the end how this relates to patient education as well as you will cover more about teaching and learning related to patient care in the summer. The big thing to remember is to know who you are communicating with, what level of communication is appropriate (i.e. medical jargon may be appropriate for certain people), and how to follow up with written communication. Documentation Document thoroughly but concisely. Remember, if you don't document that it happened, then it didn't happen. Documentation should be efficient enough to get the point across but not overly verbose. Additionally, documentation should be timely. Document communications with other healthcare providers along with patient interactions. If you have a phone conversation, follow up with a written email summarizing the communication. The written follow-up allows for a reminder, a clear line of expectations, and a written document for people to fall back on if there are questions.     Evidence Based Practice Below is a model using the principles of critical thinking and applying them to clinical decision making.

With goals identified, you will need to decide what interventions are best for the patient. The purpose of the intervention planning within treatment sessions and across treatment sessions is to bridge the gap between where the patient currently is functionally and where the patient/PT want the patient to be. It also involves constant assessments to determine if a patient is ready to progress, or if the desired outcome is not achieved, the patient needs to regress or change directions altogether.    Treating and resolving impairments alone does not mean that function is restored. Exercises need to progress to being task- specific or performed using movement patterns that closely match a patient's intended or desired functional abilities as the patient is ready. This means we need to consider a number of variables, including: Tissue quality Symptom severity Presence of co-morbidities Physical demands of ADLs and occupation   An example of task-specific training can be running. [Page 11] PHYT 6443 Hybrid Therapeutic Interventions https://softchalkcloud.com/lesson/files/ZHpaJrny25Ex1t/lesson_print.html[1/23/2026 3:35:48 PM] **This is for your practice to help you along your understanding as you progress in the course. Test material will not come from this page**   A 27-year-old graduate student was referred to Physical Therapy with acute right knee pain and swelling after an injury to the knee 1 week ago.  The patient shares that she was performing a small jump dance move and landed in an awkward way that caused her fall.  As part of the fall, she experienced an acute subluxation/reduction of her patella and a grade II injury to her MCL.  Her knee is now painful, swollen, and she is not able to walk without crutches.   The patient is an active female, and she exercises about 3-4x/week. She enjoys jogging and weightlifting. She has no prior medical history to note, no significant family medical history, and there is no chance that she is pregnant at this time. She denies smoking or illicit drug use. She has about 2 glasses of white wine per week. She has access to her program's fitness center, which is open from 6:00 am - 10:00 pm daily.   The student reports that she is extremely concerned about her success in her graduate program. She reports that this knee injury has caused her to lose a significant amount of time that she should be using to study for her qualifying exams because of all of the appointments she has had to attend. She also has difficulty sitting for prolonged periods of time needed for studying.         Examination      Evaluation Due to the acuity of the injury, you find that the patient is now presenting with the following findings: 1. Edema, which will produce loss of muscle power, motor coordination, and ROM 2. Pain, result of edema and tissue injury/intensity from the ligament tear 3. Decreased ROM, due to edema 4. Movement coordination dysfunction, due to edema, pain, altered muscle power, and altered mechanoreceptor input. If we look at secondary impairments, there is a strong correlation of patellofemoral subluxations and knee valgus injuries that occur in females, especially with hip motor coordination dysfunctions. 5. Decreased muscle power, due to edema, pain, and loss of ROM 6. Decreased balance and proprioception, due to edema, tissue injury, and altered mechanoreceptor input 7. Activity limitations with gait, transfers, bed mobility, showering/bathing, lifting 8. Participation restrictions in accessing living quarters, accessing classrooms and university services, driving, returning to dance, possibly intramural participation, possibly other recreational physical activity Diagnosis There are a lot of potential diagnoses that you could write. Here is an example: S/P traumatic grade II MCL sprain and patellofemoral subluxation producing mobility deficits.   Why mobility deficits? For right now, based on the acuity of the injury, the edema is causing pain, decreased ROM, motor coordination dysfunction, muscle power deficits, and impaired balance. A lot of this can clear up quickly once the acuity of the injury passes and once we get that swelling out of the knee to restore motion. It would also allow the patient to have decreased pain and tolerate more functional weight-bearing.   Could you have picked motor coordination as the primary deficit? Yes, it would depend on your reasoning. Knowing that the cause of the cause comes from what happened at her hip and knee with jumping, you could go down the motor coordination route. You could justify that motor coordination may improve once edema decreases, and focusing interventions quickly after that on motor coordination at the hip and knee will get the patient more quickly back to function.   
[Page 12] PHYT 6443 Hybrid Therapeutic Interventions https://softchalkcloud.com/lesson/files/ZHpaJrny25Ex1t/lesson_print.html[1/23/2026 3:35:48 PM] Could you have picked muscle power deficits? You could, but the reasoning is not as strong for right now given the acuity of the injury. To be clear, this patient's primary impairment will quickly shift to muscle power deficits once you clear up the edema and motor coordination so that you can get her back to her optimal level of function.   What I hope you take away from this point is that you can have more than one right answer. The more you develop your clinical reasoning, the easier it is to justify your reasons. The "why" aka why you choose to do something matters and should be directly related to the patients impairments.   As a general rule of thumb, taking care of the acute nature of something will take priority over working on potential causes. Example, if the whole room is on fire, putting out the candle that started it doesn't not matter at that moment but once you get the room under control, you can turn your attent better to the source of the issue. Sometimes you can go to the source to stop it from making the situation worse or making it continue, but often the acute nature of pain and edema will prevent you from effectively treating the cause. Thus you may find that treating the acute symptoms first will allow for a more successful treatment of the cause. Once the initial phase of acuity has gone down, aka the swelling has reduced and pain is manageable, you may find yourself aiming to treat the cause of the issue first and any treatments for symptoms may be used more as an adjunct to your primary treatments.   Keep in mind that we are holistic providers and should consider other resources the patient may benefit from. In this case, it may also be a good idea to refer the patient to a place like the Office of Academic and Student Affairs for accommodations in classroom settings, accessibility, etc. while the patient is recovering and still has gait deficits.   Prognosis and Plan of Care Given the acuity of the injury, there has not been time for chronic tissue changes to set in. The tissues are acting like normally inflamed tissues. The patient has an active lifestyle with no significant medical history to report. She has access to fitness facilities, and she has a higher education level. Her prognosis is good at this point.   What is her current functional status? She is ambulating with crutches with an edematous knee secondary to the injury. What is her goal functional status? She wants to be able to fully participate in classes and return to her prior level of physical activity. How do we get her there? Remember, this is going to depend on the impairment classification you selected. Based on her participation restrictions and her activity limitations, I had decided at this point in time that the mobility restriction was her primary impairment. Therefore, I would plan to initially focus on improving joint and soft tissue mobility to decrease edema, decrease pain, and improve the muscle pumping action with ROM exercises. This is also the bottom of the intervention ladder, which means it needs to be met first. Once this improves and clears out, my primary impairment would quickly change to motor coordination deficits, and I would plan to focus my interventions on motor coordination.   What would be some goals that you would write? You may post examples in the Discussion Board, and provide feedback on your classmates' goals.   Intervention We will begin to focus on different intervention strategies. You may begin to think of what you would do based on the issues you wanted to focus on. Challenge yourself to create a progression and regression for any exercise you choose. Try to increase/reduce difficulty in the same area of impairment. In other words, if the impairment is balance, adding weight-resisted exercises would not be directly related to balance. Be sure to review the Impairment Classifications from the ICF model, the goals for impairment classifications, the general treatments for the goals, and the Intervention Ladder. Suggested Break Time There is a good amount of information to process through. [Page 13] PHYT 6443 Hybrid Therapeutic Interventions https://softchalkcloud.com/lesson/files/ZHpaJrny25Ex1t/lesson_print.html[1/23/2026 3:35:48 PM] Even Dr. Shirley Sahrmann, the founder of the Movement System Impairment Syndomes, (you will cover these in ortho classes) often has questions about how long the patient should continue their exercise program. In short, the movements and interventions we prescribe are not (typically) just a short-term solution but will require life/behavior change.   So how do we help our patients unlearn faulty movement patterns or habits, learn new ways to move, and keep moving in this way? This is a multifaceted question that we will touch on but will continue to be addressed in many future classes but first we help the patient by helping them learn about their health.       This is the pdf of the results of the National Assessment of Adult Literacy. This is "nice to know" information, but it is NOT "need to know" information. You will not be tested over the contents of this pdf. It is here more for you to have access in case you want to look further into health literacy.   If this does not auto-download, you can find the file here: https://nces.ed.gov/pubsearch/pubsinfo.asp?pubid=2006483 Health Literacy Health literacy, as defined by the National Library of Medicine, is "the degree to which individuals have the capacity to obtain, process, and understand basic health information and services needed to make appropriate health decisions." This is deeper than literacy because it requires the ability to make decisions based on information presented.   The most recent assessment of Health Literacy in adults in the United States was in 2003. Unfortunately, 89% of subjects fell below "Proficient." Fortunately, 53% of subjects fell into the "Intermediate" category, which includes factors like ability to determine a healthy weight range according to BMI, ability to determine which vaccines are needed at a given age for children based on a chart, and identifying contraindications for medications based on an information pamphlet. These subjects are able to be safe, assuming information is given to them, but is the level of health literacy enough to truly make decisions? Between 2010 and 2014, additional data was collected to determine general literacy for the United States. In their findings, Texas is below average for both literacy and numeracy compared to the nation.   As PTs, we cannot overhaul health literacy on our own throughout the entire country. However, we can and should consider health literacy factors as we develop our teaching and communication skills to tailor our instructions to each patient. We need to understand each patient's health literacy level when we provide verbal and visual instructions to patients. We need to communicate in plain language, provide clear and user-friendly handouts/brochures/videos/pictures, and be mindful of our feedback to patients during sessions and between sessions. We need to constantly strive to close that gap between what we know as professionals and what the patient understands. This gives the patient ownership in their health, and it leads to longer-lasting benefits.   A common area we see issues with health literacy is related to pharmacological management of issues. Polypharmacy is unfortunately common and at the core is the assumption that patients know why they are taking medications, how to take them, what side effects to report, and what interactions may take place. While not our primary career, you may be the one who helps a person understand the importance of a medical thing (drug, surgery, procedure, etc). Don't assume someone understands, always ask and adjust as needed.   Building Rapport and Success Remember that an effective plan of care strongly benefits from a patient- therapist alliance (commonly called the Therapeutic Alliance). You will help the patient better achieve their goals by: 1. Directing care towards goals and explaining where the treatment fits in the plan to get the patient back to their goals; 2. Understanding the patient's learning style and targeting that style with instruction; 3. Understanding the patient's perceptions and attitudes towards exercise to determine how receptive the patient is to learning about and adhering to exercise (The behavior-change model); 4. Start and end with success in mind. Begin with easier exercises that the patient can achieve, and end a treatment session with success. This will help with motivation carry-over. Keep in mind a person's perception. If they don't understand why, if they have excessive pain, if they can't succeed, they will become frustrated and will not do as well no matter how "perfect" the plan of care.   For all the pedi people in the room, your patients can be a tough crowd! Kids read people very well, and they can be brutally honest!

Converting your Knowledge into Meaningful Movement: Introduction to Motor Learning Introduction to Motor Learning Motor Learning is different than Motor Performance. While Motor Performance means that the person has or develops the ability to carry out a skill, Motor Learning involves both the ability/development of ability to perform the skill as well as retain the skill. Motor learning involves complex internal processes that allow a person to perform and retain the ability to perform a skill or movement throughout practice. There are true neurologic changes that happen in the central nervous system that organize, process, and affect how motor actions are produced. In a very simple explanation, it is how we develop habits of movement or skills.   You will learn a lot more about this in Movement Science 2. The purpose of introducing these concepts in this course is to help you incorporate the basic principles into your intervention prescription and patient education. This will help you develop your patient care skills.   Types of Motor Tasks There are three different kinds of tasks that can be performed. Practice identifying the types of tasks to the right.   Discrete Tasks: These tasks involve an action or movement with a recognizable beginning and end.   Serial Tasks: These tasks involve a set or series of discrete movements that are combined in a particular sequence.   Continuous Tasks: These tasks involve repetitive, uninterrupted movement with no reasonably distinct beginning or end. Although the action may be stopped because of fatigue, the task in and of itself involves repetitions of the same movement.   Being able to recognize the type of task a patient needs to perform can help a therapist decide the best teaching methods for that specific task, pending the stages of motor learning and variables in motor learning described below.        Conditions and Progressions of Motor Tasks We do not necessarily perform the same task the same way every single time. Therefore, if we truly learn a skill, we have to be able to perform it in a variety of conditions. We need to anticipate these conditions and safely progress the patient with increased complexity of the tasks. Below is a representation of the taxonomy of motor tasks. Notice how the top left corner involves the most simple task with increased stability, no variability, no manipulation, and in a controlled environment. Changing the complexity of any of the four task dimensions makes a task more complex. You want to be sure you progress the patient, but at a rate that is appropriate for the patient.

Intertrial Variability Absent: The environment/condition in which a task occurs is constant (unchanging) from one performance to the next. This is a more simple task. Present: Demands in the environment/condition change from one performance to the next. This is a more complex task. Body Stability Body Stable: The patient keeps their body in a stable, stationary position. This is a more simple task. Body Transport: The patient is asked to move from one place to another. Object Manipulation Absent: The patient is not asked to manipulate an object, which leaves the hands, and possibly feet, pending the task, free to focus on the task requested. This is a more simple task. Present: The patient is asked to hold, carry, kick, manipulate an object. This is a more complex task.   Have a good understanding of these. [Page 1] Module 2, Part 2: Tissue Healing https://softchalkcloud.com/lesson/files/tqeYXhanRTSMPO/lesson_print.html[1/23/2026 3:37:00 PM] Module 2, Part 2: Tissue Healing   Content Overview In Ancient Egypt, honey was often used in healing. This concept is still used today when treating wounds. The special type of honey helps wounds stay moist while also naturally cleaning the wound. The content in this module is meant to help you learn how healing works and how healing principles may impact your exercise prescription. The content will not give you a perfect calendar for healing but should help guide your decision making when progressing or regressing exercises and when answering the "how long will this take?" Once you have an understanding of how healing works, think on other factors and how they may impact healing like age or smoking. Review of Tissue Healing   Task: Read Chapter 10 in your Textbook (pages 321-335).   Remember that tissues need appropriate stress to make changes. These next few pages will review the concepts of appropriate stress and safety to tissues. When prescribing exercises, we have to respect the stage of tissue healing and pain signal processing. We also have to keep in mind the goal we are trying to achieve based on the diagnosis and main impairments. Below is a review of the various phases of tissue healing.     
[Page 2] Module 2, Part 2: Tissue Healing https://softchalkcloud.com/lesson/files/tqeYXhanRTSMPO/lesson_print.html[1/23/2026 3:37:00 PM]     Acute Tissue Protection Phase (Inflammatory Phase) The first five below are the typical vascular inflammatory response that the patient will feel and may often be noted by the healthcare provider also: Swelling Redness Heat Pain at rest Loss of function Physiologically, there is clot formation and phagocytosis of cellular debris The acute phase is characterized by pain symptoms before tissue resistance with ROM testing Pain and impaired movement are from an altered chemical state that irritates nerve endings, increased tissue tension with edema and effusion, and muscle guarding. The body is trying to immobilize and protect itself. Acute inflammation typically happens for 48-72 hours, while the acute phase as a whole will typically lasts 4-6 days, unless further insult occurs. "Further insult" can be a macro trauma or a micro trauma. A macrotrauma means a big thing like a fall, slip, impact, quick reaction resulting in injury, etc. A micro trauma is more like wear and tear or continued small insult to an area. An example could be if a person sprains their ankle (macro trauma) but then continues to walk on it with an altered gait pattern (micro-trauma). Microtraumas can build up over time due to faulty movements and result in other issues (more to come in future). Goal of Intervention: Maximum Protection Control effects of inflammation (remember learning "RICE" for rest, ice, compress, elevate) Patient can and should perform nondestructive movements, like PROM, massage, and muscle setting with caution. If the injured tissue is in the lower extremity, a good rule of thumb is that if a patient cannot demonstrate normal ambulation, they need an assistive device to support weight-bearing. Keep up resistance exercises and functional activities for other tissues as long as the activity does not affect the injured tissue. Tissue should be optimally loaded. It use to be thought that if injured, then don't use it. However, all tissues, even injured, still need appropriate stress to heal. While the optimal load may be reduced from 100% to 10% or something else, it is always good to still load the tissue when possible unless loading is not safe, such as with a broken bone. Precautions: Be sure to appropriately dose interventions in this phase. Signs of too much movement or increased irritability are increased pain or increased inflammation. Think about a time that you might have rolled an ankle. If you roll it again in a day or two, the swelling becomes even more severe, indicating further injury. Muscle setting and massage should be done with the muscle/tissues in a shortened position. Contraindications: Stretching and resistance exercises should NOT be performed at the site of inflamed or swollen tissues.   
[Page 3] Module 2, Part 2: Tissue Healing https://softchalkcloud.com/lesson/files/tqeYXhanRTSMPO/lesson_print.html[1/23/2026 3:37:00 PM]     Subacute (Proliferation/Controlled Motion) Phase This phase is characterized by repair and regeneration of the tissues. Typically lasts an additional 10-17 days after the Inflammatory Phase, but can last up to 6 weeks depending on the tissue(s) that were injured. Wound closure for skin and muscle is about 5-8 days. Wound closure for tendons and ligaments is about 3-6 weeks. This is good information to keep in mind, particularly with post-operative rehabilitation. Keep in mind that there is no magic wand that says one phase starts or stops. The time frames are good to keep in mind, but look at the tissue healing and response for confirmation of appropriate healing. Additionally, note the phase overlap in the body. There is not hard stop of one phase and a switch to the other. Characterized by collagen scar formation within the tissue defect AND pain at tissue resistance. Goals: Treatment focuses on controlled load to stimulate tissue to remodel in an appropriate direction and minimize effects from immobilization. Restore ROM, then functional strength; Initiate and progress non-destructive exercises and activities. Appropriately stress the tissues within the tolerance of healing without re-injury or inflammation Precautions: In this phase, the patient feels much better, and the pain is not constant. The patient should expect to feel some discomfort as interventions and activity levels progress, but this discomfort should NOT last more than a couple of hours. Signs that there is too much motion or activity are pain at rest, fatigue in the tissues, increased weakness, and spams lasting beyond 24 hours after the movement/activity. Try not to change too many activities in the intervention. If the patient does end up with any of these signs, you might have a lot trouble figuring out which activity was the one that overdid it. Eccentric and heavy-resistance exercises, including progressive resistance exercises may cause added trauma to muscle. These exercises are typically not used in the early part of this stage because the healing tissues have weak tensile strength. Be sure to review Box 10.3 on page 328 in your textbook (7th ed).

They have a poor blood supply so they take longer). This phase includes the transition of immature, disorganized collagen to mature, organized Type 1 collagen The effects of the organization and return to pre-injury tissue structure are determined by tissue load (thus why optimal loading during all phases is important). Think about the SAID Principle from Movement Science 1 (Specific Adaptation to Imposed Demands). Differences in Maturation and Remodeling Maturation refers to the quantity of collagen stabilizing because of the balance between synthesis and degradation. The maturation comes in the quality of the collagen. This can take up to 12-18 months after the injury. Keeping in mind the SAID Principle, remember that the collagen will have maximum strength in the direction of imposed forces. Remodeling refers to the breaking of adhesions in the immature collagen molecules from the surrounding tissues. This can occur for up to 10 weeks after the injury. In this phase, the fibers NEED appropriate stress with gentle and persistent treatments to prevent adhesions and forming significant restrictions. At 14 weeks (~3.5 month), the tissue is not responsive to remodeling, and the old scar tissue poorly responds to stretching. Stretching and scar management are critical before the window closes. After this, scar modification is difficult. Pain is characterized after tissue resistance.   Goals: Progress exercises that safely stress the maturing collagen for both flexibility and strength so patient can return to prior level of function. Stretch contractures and break or mobilize adhesions. Control stresses to progress collagen tensile strength in the necessary directions. Precautions: There should be no signs of inflammation. Any discomfort that appears with activity level progressions should not last longer than a couple of hours. Signs of activities/interventions that are progressed to quickly or with too much dosage are signs of

Remodeling phases work best with appropriate loading and stressing to the tissues.       Chronic Inflammation: When Healing or Intervention Plans Go Awry   What happened? Chronic inflammation in its most simple explanation, is the prolonged or recurring pain, activity limitations, and/or participation restrictions that occur as a result of repetitive or excessive stress being imposed on tissues that were unable to respond to the stress. These are usually caused by: Overuse, cumulative trauma, or repetitive strain, which leads to repetitive strain overload over time. This will gradually weaken structures, resulting in repetitive inflammation with poor-quality tissue healing. It is very much like the phrase, "The straw that broke the camel's back." You cannot just take off the last piece of straw, and the camel stands up again. The tissues have to go through the healing process for that camel to appropriately stand up again. Trauma, especially if it is followed by superimposed repetitive trauma. The condition never had time to progress through phases of normal healing. It also may be the result of too early return to high levels of task demands. Example: You may hear about ankle sprains that "don't heal" or taking a very long time. This could be due to a missed diagnosis or the person going back to activity too soon, causing repetitive trauma to healing tissues. Reinjury of an "old scar," which is non-compliant and possibly adherent to surrounding tissues. This will alter force transmission and energy absorption in the area, making the area more susceptible to injury. Contractures or poor mobility, which lead to faulty postural habits or prolonged mobility. This can lead to tissues that have abnormal demands or have to make up for other tissues that are unable to tolerate the demands.     Task: Read about each factor on page 332 of your textbook to recognize how these factors contribute to chronic inflammation. The main thing to take away from this is to plan to address these factors in your intervention plan if your patient's symptoms are caused by chronic inflammation. This is addressing the "cause of the cause."   
[Page 8] Module 2, Part 2: Tissue Healing https://softchalkcloud.com/lesson/files/tqeYXhanRTSMPO/lesson_print.html[1/23/2026 3:37:00 PM] Intervention Planning when the Chief Complaint is Chronic Inflammation The key to treating patients who present with Chronic Inflammation as the source of their pain is to begin treating it as an acute condition. The patient is in this chronic inflammatory state because their body has not been able to move on from this state. It is like picking off a scab that just laid down over and over again. Once the inflammation is under control, the treatment can progress to dealing with impairments and functional limitations. Start with the following from Box 10.5 on page 333 of your textbook. In this phase, you want to focus on patient education which is not easy. Often, you will need to de-educate the patient. This means you need to respectfully and professionally challenge the beliefs and understandings of the patient to help them get on the right path. An example of this would be the common saying of "no pain, no gain." Many believe they need to push themselves and the more it hurts, the more it is healing. Helping a patient understand this is not the best path may be difficult if they have used this mindset their whole life and especially if it has helped them in the past. Through listening, empathy, and compassion (and time) these beliefs can be altered. Additionally, there is a stigma against patients with chronic inflammation, and the patient's tend to not trust providers. The patients often have been told their symptoms are not real or "should have healed" which implies it is the patient's fault. Providers may also at times imply that the patient is "crazy" or is only being seen to seek medication, financial gain, or avoidance of tasks like work. It is highly recommended that you take time at the beginning of your intervention plan to educate and illustrate what is happening to the patient. It is not uncommon for the first sessions to be mostly talking. This allows the patient to understand that while feeling that much pain is not normal for anyone, it is common and that they are in the right place for care. Educate the patient so that they can feel like they have control over their health again. Help the patient visualize that the first thing that needs to happen is that you are going to help them quit irritating those tissues.   For interventions, start with only nonstressful activities. It is critical with these patients that you have them start with success. This will decrease the pain alarms that have developed over time, and it will increase your chance of helping this patient get back to loving their lives. Additionally, it is important to get the tissue right. AKA what is the tissue that is angry? Nerve, tendon, muscle, joint, etc.? You will develop your skills of identification over the rest of your program. For now, start in the safe zone for all tissues which are low intensity, slow motions, low load, etc. all the things for acute injuries.   Once the inflammation calms down, the rest of Box 10.5 in your textbook can be best used. The main focus is still controlling the stresses until the connective tissues involved develop the ability to withstand stresses imposed by the functional activities.

If it is a muscle tear, you know from earlier that this should come together in ~8- 10 days. We know from earlier that these can take much longer to come together so if you started AAROM on day 14 with a tendon tear, you would potentially cause stress/harm to the healing tissue. You won't always know the injured tissue but through good differential diagnosis you can make a strong hypothesis. You will learn more of these skills over your program.   Severity of Tissue Injury To give you an idea of the extent of damage, remember the following grades. The higher the grade, the more complete the tear. The more complete the tear, the longer the "healing clock" will be. Expect injuries at a higher degree to require more protection initially, more time for proliferation and repair, and a longer time needed to return the patient back to function. Grade 1 (first degree): Mild pain at the time of injury or within the first 24 hours. Mild swelling, local tenderness, and pain occur when the tissue is stressed. Grade 2 (second degree): Moderate pain that requires stopping the activity. Stress and palpation of the tissue greatly increase the pain. When the injury is to ligaments, some of the fibers are torn, resulting in some increased joint mobility. Definitely plan to include motor control to help control this excessive motion. Grade 3 (third degree): Near-complete or complete tear or avulsion of the tissue (tendon or ligament) with severe pain. Stress to the torn tissue is usually painless (the nerves are damaged), palpation may reveal the defect. A torn ligament results in instability of the joint. [Page 12] Module 2, Part 2: Tissue Healing https://softchalkcloud.com/lesson/files/tqeYXhanRTSMPO/lesson_print.html[1/23/2026 3:37:00 PM]   Mechanotransduction   This information is not in your textbook, but it is "need to know" information!   Mechanotransduction is the mechanism by which the cells convert mechanical stimuli into cellular responses. For example, when we appropriately load a healing tissue, mechanotransduction is the process that we are trying to tap into to promote effective tissue healing. It is an ongoing physiologic process.   It is considered to be composed of 3 steps: 1. Mechanocoupling: the direct or indirect physical perturbation of the cell through shear or compression that is transformed into chemical signals within and among cells 2. Cell-to-cell communication – transfer of signaling from one cell to another 3. Effector response – biochemical signals that influence gene expression in the nucleus and promote intracellular processes leading to matrix remodeling     Long story short, mechanotransduction describes how the musculoskeletal system generates, absorbs, and transmits force. The mechanical forces introduced on the musculoskeletal system direct cellular activity, which results in adaptations to mechanical demands. It is a "super sciency" application of the SAID principle!   If you are interested in learning more about these cellular responses to mechanical stimuli, I highly recommend reading the article below. Flexibility is the ability to rotate a single joint or series of joints smoothly and easily through an unrestricted, pain free range of motion (ROM). It is influenced by: Viscoelastic properties of the muscle-tendon unit Some people's musculotendinous junctions just have more viscoelasticity, which helps them be more flexible. Skin and neurovascular structures Nerves can and should move a little bit, but they don't like to move! Some people's nerves (Dr. Vargo's included) are short and wound up! You do not want to hold a prolonged stretch on nerves because they need a lot of oxygen to function normally. Holding a nerve stretch lessens the oxygen and nutrition flow, which is rough for the nerve. Joint capsule and ligaments An example of this is in the shoulder joint capsule. The posterior capsule of the shoulder joint tends to be thicker, and response to surgery or adhesive capsulitis means more collagen lays down, making the joint capsule thicker. This is why it is so difficult to mobilize into glenohumeral flexion and internal rotation. Bony morphology Think back to testing hip internal and external rotation range of motion. Some of you had limited internal rotation and excessive external rotation, but the arc of motion was still equal to 90°. [Page 2] PHYT 6443: Hybrid Therapeutic Interventions https://softchalkcloud.com/lesson/files/jXUhPZurLyfdC7/lesson_print.html[1/25/2026 5:33:45 PM] When we assess joint mobility, we are assessing the joint play, which is "involuntary movement that is present in all synovial joints. Voluntary movement is dependent on the integrity of joint play." (Menell)   Therefore, appropriate amounts of joint play support normal gross movement for joints. Joint play should only be about 1/8 of an inch, so it is very small overall. With appropriate practice and training, you can still feel about 1/8 of an inch of movement. Fair warning, just like surface anatomy and palpation, it is a skill/art that must be practiced. The lab videos will show you how to assess, and then turn those assessments into treatments. We will continue to practice and reinforce these skills throughout the remainder of the program.   When a joint is in dysfunction (doesn't move how it normally should), it can lead to either hypermobility or hypomobility. What is important to remember is that if joint play is lost, it is a mechanical problem that requires a mechanical solution. The change in accessory motion, by the very definition of accessory motion, cannot be restored with muscle activation.     Joint Mobilizations Mobilizations are passive, skilled manual therapy techniques applied to joints using physiological or accessory motions. Mobilizations are graded according to the amplitude and depth of mobilizations, often using the Grade I-IV approach. Grades I/II are better for pain modulation and fluid imbibition. Grades III/IV are better for improving joint mobility.   Grading Joint Mobilizations So what do I mean by Grade I/II versus Grade III/IV. These are called Maitland Mobilization grades. These grades refer to the amount of mobility in any given direction for a joint. You have to know what Grade IV is for an individual's joint in a specific direction to know how to implement Grade I, II, or III. This does not mean get a running start. Rather, gradually work into Grade IV with your assessment. To help you with this, I am posting a video description of the mobilizations and a picture to help with visualization. I will repeat these descriptions later in the module to try to help reinforce and tie the information together.

Intended to alter positional relationships, snap adhesions, or stimulate joint receptors. Best used when there is a loss of joint mobility in order to restore mobility at that segment. May hear a little snap, crackle, pop, but this is just nitrogen gas rapidly being released from a joint. We will learn specific manipulations for joints beginning next year. I want you to get good at mobilizations before we turn to manipulations.   These may also be called Grade V mobilizations in the clinic when you are on rotation.   Did you know? This means that you need to be aware of all the arthrokinematics within the body to effectively apply joint mobilizations. For example, if you push something in the wrong direction, you could make their condition worse. Typically, if you know the "glide" component of the arthrokinematics, that is the direction that you will push. Example: The glenohumeral joint rolls superior and glides inferior with shoulder ABduction with normal motion. Thus, if there is a joint issue and the person is found have limitations with Abduction, you as a PT would look to push the join in the direction that it would normally glide AKA in an inferior direction. The good news is that you do not need to memorize every single joint because all joints follow the typical arthrokinematics principles of roll-glide-spin based on their structure. Knowing that, you only need to know the joint structure and then apply your typical convex-concave/other rules.

You are inducing the glide component of the technique. On joints that are concave on convex, whatever direction you are moving a bone is in the same direction of osteokinematic movement. On joints that are convex on concave, whatever direction you are moving a bone is in the opposite direction of osteokinematic movement.   For example, let's consider the glenohumeral joint. The humeral head is convex, and the glenoid fossa is concave. For shoulder abduction, the humeral head rolls superior and glides inferior. When you assess inferior glide of the humeral head, it is associated with glenohumeral abduction.       Documenting Joint Mobility Findings If you assess joint mobility and findings, you would document these findings in the objective portion of the test. Introduction to Lumbar Spine I. The Manual Therapy Institute, PLLC; 2022. Muscle Energy Techniques (METs): Where joint mobilizations and muscle activation combines!   What are musculoskeletal METs? A muscle energy technique (MET) refers to any mobilization that involves the voluntary use of the patient's muscles. Think about it like PNF combined with joint mobilizations (see videos of soft tissue lower extremity techniques).     How METs came to be? The technique was developed by Dr. Fred Mitchell, Sr., DO (osteopath) and Dr. Fred Mitchell, Jr., DO around 1950. The thought process behind this procedure was that muscles become hypertonic, which can restrict the osteokinematic function of the motion segment. To restore joint mobility, the technique involves accurate localization of the motion barrier and specific muscle activation to encourage a joint to move either into the barrier of motion or away from the barrier of motion. Osteopaths in the United States currently practice and treat more like MDs in the United States in order to keep up their practice and medical privileges. Osteopaths in Europe and a few decades ago in the USA practice more like PTs do now.  The image to the right was taken in 2016 in Ontario, Canada. These techniques are still new when we think about the science and art of Physical Therapy. The techniques will take some practice because it involves gentle patient handling, but they are very useful tools to aid your clinical planning.   
[Page 12] PHYT 6443: Hybrid Therapeutic Interventions https://softchalkcloud.com/lesson/files/jXUhPZurLyfdC7/lesson_print.html[1/25/2026 5:33:45 PM] Example of an MET Asking the Agonist to Work Directions: For side glide right. Can be done in neutral, semi-flexion/ extension. Side glide head to right up to restriction barrier, which creates left side bending. Instruct patient to push their head gently to their right side. The therapist manually resists for 6-8 seconds. Then pick up slack in right side gliding. Perform 3-4 repetitions. Notice how the right cervical spine muscles are being asked to contract in a lengthened position with this technique. How do you know which muscle to use? 
[Page 13] PHYT 6443: Hybrid Therapeutic Interventions https://softchalkcloud.com/lesson/files/jXUhPZurLyfdC7/lesson_print.html[1/25/2026 5:33:45 PM] Asking the Antagonist to Work There are some METs that you will perform where you want to contract the muscles that are antagonists to the muscle with dysfunction. The idea is that contraction of the antagonist muscle will cause the muscle in dysfunction to relax. With this kind of technique, it is very important that you palpate the muscle in dysfunction to ensure that the force is not so great that you cause a stabilization reaction. As we go through a few key muscle energy techniques in this course and in Ortho, I will help you figure out whether you are asking the agonist or antagonist to work.  Asking the Agonist to Work Likewise, you can perform an MET where you ask the patient to contract the muscle in dysfunction from a lengthened position. The idea behind this is the force produced by the muscle will activate the Golgi tendon organs, which, as you know from Neuroscience, causes reflexive inhibition to the muscle. We will practice identifying the agonist and antagonist more in immersion.   Review of Dosing Clinical Summary METs are excellent tools to have in your back pocket. Practice them, and use them frequently, especially if the end feel of a joint is predominately muscular or the patient has difficulty relaxing. Regular practice of joint mobilizations and METs is critical for their success. The wind-up has to be very localized with precise resistance provided to the patient to ensure the correct muscle is activating without other muscles "jumping in to help." It takes confidence and skills with palpation. You may not feel anything in the beginning but as your hands develope, you will be able to sense small changes in tissue movement.

[Page 16] Modual 4: Introduction to Motor Coordination https://softchalkcloud.com/lesson/files/19BHjp8msnTMA6/lesson_print.html[3/27/2026 11:11:08 AM]           Evidence of Motor Learning & Improved Motor Control: To determine the success of motor learning and skilled performance the following concepts are used: Improvement – measuring the skill i.e. archery score, more accurately hitting a target. There should be a decrease in errors. Consistency – the individual is able to reliably produce the result Retention – Retention tests are used following a delay in practice to determine if permanent learning has occurred. Transfer – The ability to perform a similar movement, within a different context in which it was initially learnt is tested (transfer) i.e. changing the amount of force or surface, location etc (Muratori, Lamberg, Quinn & Duff, 2013). During rehabilitation, progressing an exercise subsequently increases the amount of information to be processed. If the progression is added too quickly, the ability to understand the information may not have concurrently increased (Guadagnoli and Lee, 2004).        Questions to Consider & Ponder: How can I best structure practice (therapeutic session) to ensure learning occurs? How can I ensure that skills learned in one context will transfer to another (home)? Will simplifying a task result in more efficient learning?   Looking ahead: We will jump in to learn exercise interventions to optimize motor coordination of the spine, upper and lower quarters.       Additional References: 1. [Page 1] TREATMENT STRATEGY: MOTOR CONTROL 
[Page 2] OBJECTIVES • Identify the target outcome of motor control intervention • Discuss indication for motor coordination interventions along the intervention ladder. (see slide 5) • Analyze movement interventions with intent to determine which muscle performance treatment strategy(ies) is/are dominant. (see slide 7) • Prescribe a patient specific exercise intervention, including mode, intensity, duration, and frequency, to decrease symptoms, improve mobility, motor coordination, and/or stability. • Recognize the variable motor coordination strategies present in basic functional movements. • Discuss with colleague additional motor control exercises (at least 3 per category) to appropriately categorize strategy and expand bank of exercise interventions. (Bring to Immersion) 
[Page 3] PHYSICAL THERAPY DIAGNOSIS DRIVES THE TREATMENT STRATEGY • Primary mobility deficits – Treatment strategy is to restore motion to restricted joint • Primary motor coordination deficits – Treatment strategy is to improve muscle performance with emphasis on stability and reduction of shear stress to joint system • Primary sensory selection deficits – Treatment strategy to target sensory selection and re-weighting from somatosensory, visual, and/or vestibular systems • Primary force production deficits – Treatment strategy is to increase muscle strength and endurance • Primary aerobic capacity deficits – Treatment strategy to improve function of cardiopulmonary system and metabolic efficiency 
[Page 4] WHAT MODE OF EXERCISE DO WE PRESCRIBE? • To increase joint range of motion • joint mobilization (capsular stretch), imbibition (intra-articular inflammation) • To increase muscle flexibility • stretching, myofascial release/soft tissue mobilization • To increase continuous muscular output • Muscle endurance training, cardiovascular training • To increase muscle force generation • Muscle resistance/strengthening training • To increase/improve motor recruitment and skill acquisition • motor coordination training • To reduce extracellular edema • tissue evacuation • To reduce pain • pain signal modulation 
[Page 5] EXERCISE INTERVENTION LADDER Pain & Edema Modulation Mobility Motor coordination Muscle Endurance/ Strength Muscle power Complex Functional Activities requiring agility, power, speed Need to regain pain free range of motion prior to implementing progressive loading to functional movement patterns 
[Page 6] INDICATIONS FOR MOTOR CONTROL INTERVENTIONS • Aberrant Movement • Delayed recruitment, poor dissociation, lack of smooth/efficient motion • Episodic Pain Complaint • Symptom Reduction with External Support • Post Mobility Interventions • Intolerance to Prolonged Static Postures • Reports of Fatigue/'head feels heavy' • Frequent Self-Manipulation • Presence of hypermobile joint play or general hypermobile ROM • Observed Hinge Points in Spinal ROM 
[Page 7] MOTOR COORDINATION (IMPROVING MUSCLE PERFORMANCE) • Improving muscle performance treatment strategies: • Exercises that target controlled mobility (typically associated with multiple joint segments such as spine) • Exercises that target a specific muscle synergy group • Exercises that target a specific muscle (reduce dominance of one muscle group in the synergy) • Exercises that target co-contraction (agonist/antagonist co-contraction – aka stability) • Exercises that target dissociation of movement of one region or joint system without movement or compensation at another part of the kinetic chain • Exercise that target a coordinated reaction to perturbation Goal: functional movement efficiency to meet the physical demand of task

[Page 24] DECISION MAKING: DOSAGE Overall Theme: High volume (reps), Low load (resistance) • Intensity: Moderate level of challenge. ‘Intense enough’ to require attention but needs to be successful. If pain is involved in the poor motor map, we need the patient to perform the skill with good form and no pain (pain alters the motor selection). We can adjust intensity through sensory input or complexity of the skill. • Duration: what they can do well; science tells us 10-15 minutes of practice time induces neuroplastic changes of motor learning. • Frequency: multiple times per day 
[Page 25] CASE APPLICATION: • A 17 year old male presents to your clinic s/p ACL-R, quadriceps tendon allograft 1 week ago. His current post operative restrictions include WBAT with hinge knee brace extension lock when weight bearing. The patient shares his chief complaint is soreness of his right knee, stiffness, and weakness in his right leg as he reports heaviness when trying to lift his leg and when walking. • Where do you start?

[Page 27] HOW DOES THIS INFORMATION GUIDE US? Body Structure/Function Impairments • Edema • Range of motion restriction • Delayed recruitment of quadriceps • Weakness of GMax, GMed, Hip ERs Activity Restrictions • Independent Gait • Transfers (sit<>stand, car) • Stair mobility • Single limb stance stability • Hopping • Running • Cutting Participation Limitations • Tennis: Singles/Doubles • Driving • Snowboarding 
[Page 28] PT DIAGNOSES: PRIMARY MOBILITY DEFICIT WITH CONCURRENT MOTOR COORDINATION DYSFUNCTION. • Primary Mobility Deficits • Treatment strategy: To restore joint range of motion at tibiofemoral/patellofemoral/proximal tibiofibular joints • Mode: Joint mobilization, PROM/AAROM/AROM, Soft tissue mobilization • Motor Coordination Dysfunction • Treatment strategy: To restore motor recruitment efficiency • Mode: Motor control/coordination training (Neuromuscular Re-education) • Example Prescription: Long sitting quadriceps isometric (quad set) • 3x20 repetitions with a 2 second hold • 3 times/day 
[Page 29] SUMMARY • Motor Control: We are trying to improve the recruitment of muscle synergies to control and improve the precision of motion to perform or complete a more complex task that requires the coordination. • We are trying to reduce the variability of movement to make movement more efficient and effective. • We can manipulate sensory input to affect the selection and response of the motor system. • Big picture, we are training the brain (selection and execution/coordination of movement from the CNS). How do I know I accomplished the goal? The salient sign is improved coordination of movement (although you may not see this within session).
